main_paper.raw: {"methods":["SMI-TED: SMILE Transformer Encoder Decoder, a large-scale encoder-decoder transformer model for molecular data","Self-supervised pre-training on 91 million SMILES samples (4 billion molecular tokens) from PubChem","Two model variants: base (289M parameters) and Mixture-of-Experts (8 × 289M parameters)","Fine-tuning on downstream tasks including property prediction and reaction yield prediction","Evaluation of latent space compositionality and few-shot learning capabilities"],"problems":["Accurate prediction of quantum chemical, physical, biophysical, and physiological properties of small molecules","Prediction of reaction yields for chemical reactions","Efficient and scalable molecular representation learning for downstream tasks","Overcoming the limitations of traditional, labor-intensive experimental methods in materials and chemistry"],"datasets":["91 million SMILES samples from PubChem (for pre-training)","11 benchmark datasets for classification and regression tasks (covering quantum mechanical, physical, biophysical, and physiological properties)","MOSES benchmarking dataset (for molecule reconstruction)","Pd-catalyzed Buchwald–Hartwig C-N cross-coupling reaction dataset (for reaction yield prediction)"],"metrics":["State-of-the-art performance (relative to benchmarks)","Molecule reconstruction accuracy (MOSES)","Efficiency of molecular latent space (compositionality, separability)","Few-shot learning performance"],"results":[{"metric":"State-of-the-art performance","value":"Achieved across multiple benchmark datasets for molecular property prediction and reaction yield prediction"},{"metric":"Molecule reconstruction accuracy (MOSES)","value":"Demonstrated high reconstruction capacity (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Latent space compositionality and separability","value":"Latent space exhibits strong compositionality and separability, supporting higher-level reasoning and few-shot learning"}],"novelty_claims":["First large-scale encoder-decoder foundation model for materials and chemistry trained on 91 million SMILES samples (4 billion tokens)","Two scalable model variants (base and Mixture-of-Experts) for flexible scientific applications","Demonstrates state-of-the-art results across a wide range of molecular property and reaction yield prediction tasks","Latent space exhibits compositionality and separability, enabling higher-level reasoning and few-shot learning","Model weights and source code are made publicly available to facilitate further research"]}

methods: SMI-TED: SMILE Transformer Encoder Decoder, a large-scale encoder-decoder transformer model for molecular data
Self-supervised pre-training on 91 million SMILES samples (4 billion molecular tokens) from PubChem
Two model variants: base (289M parameters) and Mixture-of-Experts (8 × 289M parameters)
Fine-tuning on downstream tasks including property prediction and reaction yield prediction
Evaluation of latent space compositionality and few-shot learning capabilities
problems: Accurate prediction of quantum chemical, physical, biophysical, and physiological properties of small molecules
Prediction of reaction yields for chemical reactions
Efficient and scalable molecular representation learning for downstream tasks
Overcoming the limitations of traditional, labor-intensive experimental methods in materials and chemistry
datasets: 91 million SMILES samples from PubChem (for pre-training)
11 benchmark datasets for classification and regression tasks (covering quantum mechanical, physical, biophysical, and physiological properties)
Pd-catalyzed Buchwald–Hartwig C-N cross-coupling reaction dataset (for reaction yield prediction)
metrics: Efficiency of molecular latent space (compositionality, separability)

results.value: Achieved across multiple benchmark datasets for molecular property prediction and reaction yield prediction
Demonstrated high reconstruction capacity (exact value not specified)
Latent space exhibits strong compositionality and separability, supporting higher-level reasoning and few-shot learning

novelty_claims: First large-scale encoder-decoder foundation model for materials and chemistry trained on 91 million SMILES samples (4 billion tokens)
Two scalable model variants (base and Mixture-of-Experts) for flexible scientific applications
Demonstrates state-of-the-art results across a wide range of molecular property and reaction yield prediction tasks
Latent space exhibits compositionality and separability, enabling higher-level reasoning and few-shot learning
Model weights and source code are made publicly available to facilitate further research

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Generative Molecular Transformer (GMTransformer), a probabilistic neural network model for generative design of molecules","Blank filling language model adapted from text processing for molecular generation"],"problems":["Generative design of organic molecules","Interpretability of deep learning models for molecule design","Data efficiency in molecular generative models"],"datasets":["MOSES datasets"],"metrics":["Novelty","Scaf (Scaffold similarity)"],"results":[{"metric":"Novelty","value":"High compared to other baselines"},{"metric":"Scaf","value":"High compared to other baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["First application of a blank filling language model to molecular generative design, leveraging its interpretability and data efficiency.","Probabilistic generation steps allow for interpretable molecule modification recommendations, guided by learned implicit chemistry.","GMTransformer achieves high novelty and scaffold similarity compared to existing baselines."]}

methods: Generative Molecular Transformer (GMTransformer), a probabilistic neural network model for generative design of molecules
Blank filling language model adapted from text processing for molecular generation
problems: Generative design of organic molecules
Interpretability of deep learning models for molecule design
Data efficiency in molecular generative models

results.value: High compared to other baselines
High compared to other baselines

novelty_claims: First application of a blank filling language model to molecular generative design, leveraging its interpretability and data efficiency.
Probabilistic generation steps allow for interpretable molecule modification recommendations, guided by learned implicit chemistry.
GMTransformer achieves high novelty and scaffold similarity compared to existing baselines.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["GP-MoLFormer: an autoregressive molecular string generator based on a 46.8M parameter transformer decoder with linear attention and rotary positional encodings","Pair-tuning: a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method using property-ordered molecular pairs for property-guided optimization"],"problems":["General-purpose molecular generation from large-scale chemical datasets","De novo molecular generation","Scaffold-constrained molecular decoration","Unconstrained property-guided molecular optimization","Understanding and quantifying memorization and novelty in molecular generation models"],"datasets":["Over 1.1 billion chemical SMILES (large-scale chemical dataset)"],"metrics":["Performance on de novo generation","Performance on scaffold-constrained decoration","Performance on property-guided optimization","Memorization rate","Novelty of generated molecules"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance across all three tasks (de novo, scaffold-constrained, property-guided optimization)","value":"GP-MoLFormer performs better or comparable to baselines"},{"metric":"Memorization rate","value":"Strong memorization of training data observed"},{"metric":"Novelty of generations","value":"Novelty is impacted by training data quality and scale; duplication bias lowers novelty"}],"novelty_claims":["First to train a transformer-based molecular generator on over 1.1 billion chemical SMILES for generative tasks","Introduction of pair-tuning, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for property-guided optimization using property-ordered molecular pairs","Comprehensive evaluation of a single model across de novo generation, scaffold-constrained decoration, and property-guided optimization without additional training for the first two tasks","First to systematically analyze and report strong memorization in chemical language models and its relationship with training data quality, scale, and duplication bias","Establishment of a scaling law relating inference compute and novelty in molecular generations"]}

methods: GP-MoLFormer: an autoregressive molecular string generator based on a 46.8M parameter transformer decoder with linear attention and rotary positional encodings
Pair-tuning: a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method using property-ordered molecular pairs for property-guided optimization
problems: General-purpose molecular generation from large-scale chemical datasets
De novo molecular generation
Scaffold-constrained molecular decoration
Unconstrained property-guided molecular optimization
Understanding and quantifying memorization and novelty in molecular generation models
datasets: Over 1.1 billion chemical SMILES (large-scale chemical dataset)

results.metric: Performance across all three tasks (de novo, scaffold-constrained, property-guided optimization)
results.value: GP-MoLFormer performs better or comparable to baselines
Strong memorization of training data observed
Novelty is impacted by training data quality and scale; duplication bias lowers novelty

novelty_claims: First to train a transformer-based molecular generator on over 1.1 billion chemical SMILES for generative tasks
Introduction of pair-tuning, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for property-guided optimization using property-ordered molecular pairs
Comprehensive evaluation of a single model across de novo generation, scaffold-constrained decoration, and property-guided optimization without additional training for the first two tasks
First to systematically analyze and report strong memorization in chemical language models and its relationship with training data quality, scale, and duplication bias
Establishment of a scaling law relating inference compute and novelty in molecular generations

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["MOLFormer: an efficient transformer encoder model with rotary positional embeddings and linear attention mechanism, trained on SMILES sequences of molecules using highly distributed training"],"problems":["Accurate and fast molecular property prediction","Limited availability of property labels for supervised learning in chemistry","Capturing molecular structure and properties from large unlabelled chemical datasets"],"datasets":["PubChem (1.1 billion unlabelled molecules)","ZINC (1.1 billion unlabelled molecules)","Ten benchmark datasets for downstream property prediction tasks"],"metrics":["Performance on downstream property prediction tasks (e.g., accuracy, ROC-AUC, RMSE, depending on the specific task)"],"results":[{"metric":"Downstream property prediction performance","value":"Outperforms existing baselines, including supervised and self-supervised graph neural networks and language models, on several tasks from ten benchmark datasets; competitive on two others"}],"novelty_claims":["First large-scale transformer-based language model (MOLFormer) trained on over 1.1 billion unlabelled molecules for molecular property prediction","Use of rotary positional embeddings and linear attention for efficient large-scale training on chemical SMILES","Demonstration that large-scale molecular language models can capture spatial relationships between atoms and predict diverse molecular properties, including quantum-chemical properties"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Uni-Mol+: a method that generates raw 3D molecular conformations from 1D/2D data using RDKit, then iteratively updates these conformations towards DFT equilibrium using neural networks, and predicts quantum chemical properties from the learned conformation","Two-track Transformer model backbone with atom and pair representation tracks, enhanced with 3D spatial and graph positional encoding, outer product, triangular update, and feed-forward network","Novel training strategy that samples conformations from a pseudo trajectory between raw and equilibrium conformations to address distributional shift and improve learning"],"problems":["Accurate and efficient prediction of quantum chemical (QC) properties without expensive electronic structure calculations like DFT","Overcoming the limitations of previous deep learning methods that use 1D SMILES or 2D molecular graphs, which do not capture the 3D equilibrium conformations critical for QC properties"],"datasets":["PCQM4MV2","Open Catalyst 2020 (OC20)"],"metrics":["L1 loss (mean absolute error)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Uni-Mol+, which iteratively updates raw 3D conformations towards DFT equilibrium using neural networks, rather than directly predicting from 1D/2D data","Introduces a two-track Transformer backbone with enhanced pair representation and iterative 3D coordinate updates","Designs a novel training strategy using pseudo trajectory sampling to better learn the mapping from raw to equilibrium conformations and address distributional shift","Demonstrates significant improvement over previous state-of-the-art methods on public QC property prediction benchmarks"]}

methods: Uni-Mol+: a method that generates raw 3D molecular conformations from 1D/2D data using RDKit, then iteratively updates these conformations towards DFT equilibrium using neural networks, and predicts quantum chemical properties from the learned conformation
Two-track Transformer model backbone with atom and pair representation tracks, enhanced with 3D spatial and graph positional encoding, outer product, triangular update, and feed-forward network
Novel training strategy that samples conformations from a pseudo trajectory between raw and equilibrium conformations to address distributional shift and improve learning
problems: Accurate and efficient prediction of quantum chemical (QC) properties without expensive electronic structure calculations like DFT
Overcoming the limitations of previous deep learning methods that use 1D SMILES or 2D molecular graphs, which do not capture the 3D equilibrium conformations critical for QC properties
novelty_claims: Proposes Uni-Mol+, which iteratively updates raw 3D conformations towards DFT equilibrium using neural networks, rather than directly predicting from 1D/2D data
Introduces a two-track Transformer backbone with enhanced pair representation and iterative 3D coordinate updates
Designs a novel training strategy using pseudo trajectory sampling to better learn the mapping from raw to equilibrium conformations and address distributional shift
Demonstrates significant improvement over previous state-of-the-art methods on public QC property prediction benchmarks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SMILES-to-Property-Transformer (SPT): a transformer-based natural language processing model to predict binary limiting activity coefficients from SMILES codes","Two-step training: pretraining on large synthetic dataset from COSMO-RS, followed by fine-tuning on experimental data"],"problems":["Accurate prediction of limiting activity coefficients for binary mixtures, especially for unknown molecules","Lack of sufficient experimental data for activity coefficients due to high cost of experiments","Poor extrapolation of existing machine learning models to unknown molecules"],"datasets":["Synthetic dataset generated from COSMO-RS (10 million data points)","Experimental dataset (20,870 data points)"],"metrics":["Mean prediction error"],"results":[{"metric":"Mean prediction error","value":"Cut in half compared to state-of-the-art models (COSMO-RS, UNIFACDortmund) and improved over recent machine learning approaches"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a SMILES-to-Property-Transformer (SPT) model for predicting limiting activity coefficients from SMILES using a transformer architecture","Two-step training strategy: pretraining on synthetic data and fine-tuning on experimental data to overcome data scarcity and improve extrapolation","Demonstration that SPT achieves higher accuracy and better extrapolation to unknown molecules than previous state-of-the-art models and machine learning approaches"]}

methods: UniMAP: a universal SMILES-graph representation learning model
Embedding layer for token and node/edge representation in SMILES and graph
Multi-layer Transformer for deep cross-modality fusion
Four pre-training tasks: Multi-Level Cross-Modality Masking (CMM), SMILES-Graph Matching (SGM), Fragment-Level Alignment (FLA), Domain Knowledge Learning (DKL)
BRICS algorithm for fragment extraction
SMILES-graph fragment decomposition algorithm
problems: Limited use of single molecular modality (either SMILES or graph) in existing molecular pre-training models
Inability to capture fine-grained semantics between SMILES and graph representations
Failure of global-level alignment methods to recognize subtle molecular differences that affect properties
Need for comprehensive cross-modality fusion for molecular representation learning
datasets: MoleculeNet (for molecular property prediction, including HIV dataset)
metrics: Performance on molecular property prediction tasks (e.g., ROC-AUC, accuracy, etc. as typical in MoleculeNet)

results.value: State-of-the-art (exact numbers not provided)
Outperforms existing methods (exact numbers not provided)
Outperforms existing methods (exact numbers not provided)

novelty_claims: UniMAP is the first single-stream multi-modality molecular representation learning method leveraging both SMILES and graph representations.
Design of both molecular-level (SGM and DKL) and fragment-level (CMM and FLA) pre-training tasks for deep cross-modality fusion.
Integration of fragment-level alignment and masking to capture fine-grained cross-modality semantics between SMILES and graph.
Demonstration of superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on multiple downstream tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Differential Reaction Fingerprint (DRFP): a binary fingerprint generated from the symmetric difference of circular molecular n-grams from molecules on each side of the reaction arrow in a reaction SMILES, without distinguishing between reactants and reagents"],"problems":["Reaction classification: automating the classification of chemical reactions into classes","Reaction yield prediction: predicting the percentage yield of chemical reactions","Limitations of existing methods: need for large training datasets, bias, complexity, and lack of interpretability in deep learning-based learned fingerprints; computational cost and lack of generalizability in DFT- and structure-based fingerprints"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Yield prediction performance (e.g., R^2, MAE, but not specified in the provided text)"],"results":[{"metric":"Reaction classification accuracy (DRFP vs. structure-based fingerprints)","value":"DRFP outperforms structure-based fingerprints"},{"metric":"Reaction yield prediction performance (DRFP vs. DFT-based fingerprints)","value":"DRFP outperforms DFT-based fingerprints"},{"metric":"Comparison to learned fingerprints","value":"DRFP reaches the performance of state-of-the-art learned fingerprints in both tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of the Differential Reaction Fingerprint (DRFP), a data-independent, interpretable, and computationally efficient fingerprint for chemical reactions","DRFP does not require distinguishing between reactants and reagents, atom-mapping, or calculation of molecular properties for reagents","DRFP achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art learned fingerprints for reaction classification and yield prediction without the need for large training datasets or complex deep learning architectures"]}

methods: Differential Reaction Fingerprint (DRFP): a binary fingerprint generated from the symmetric difference of circular molecular n-grams from molecules on each side of the reaction arrow in a reaction SMILES, without distinguishing between reactants and reagents
problems: Reaction classification: automating the classification of chemical reactions into classes
Reaction yield prediction: predicting the percentage yield of chemical reactions

Limitations of existing methods: need for large training datasets, bias, complexity, and lack of interpretability in deep learning-based learned fingerprints; computational cost and lack of generalizability in DFT- and structure-based fingerprints
metrics: Yield prediction performance (e.g., R^2, MAE, but not specified in the provided text)

results.metric: Reaction classification accuracy (DRFP vs. structure-based fingerprints)
results.value: DRFP outperforms structure-based fingerprints

results.metric: Reaction yield prediction performance (DRFP vs. DFT-based fingerprints)
results.value: DRFP outperforms DFT-based fingerprints
DRFP reaches the performance of state-of-the-art learned fingerprints in both tasks

novelty_claims: Introduction of the Differential Reaction Fingerprint (DRFP), a data-independent, interpretable, and computationally efficient fingerprint for chemical reactions
DRFP does not require distinguishing between reactants and reagents, atom-mapping, or calculation of molecular properties for reagents

DRFP achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art learned fingerprints for reaction classification and yield prediction without the need for large training datasets or complex deep learning architectures

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Junction Tree Variational Autoencoder (JT-VAE) for molecular graph generation","Two-phase generation: (1) generate a tree-structured scaffold (junction tree) of chemical substructures, (2) assemble subgraphs into a molecular graph using a graph message passing network"],"problems":["Automated design of molecules with specific chemical properties","Continuous embedding and generation of molecular graphs","Direct generation of molecular graphs (as opposed to SMILES string generation)","Ensuring chemical validity during molecular graph generation","Efficient optimization of molecular properties"],"datasets":["Not explicitly named in the provided content, but mentions 'training set' of molecules and standard datasets used in prior work (e.g., those used by Kusner et al., 2017; Dai et al., 2018)"],"metrics":["Molecule validity (percentage of valid molecules generated)","Property optimization success (relative gain in discovering molecules with desired properties)"],"results":[{"metric":"Molecule validity","value":"100% valid molecules when sampled from prior"},{"metric":"Property optimization gain","value":"30% relative gain over baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["First direct generative model for molecular graphs using a junction tree representation, rather than SMILES strings","Two-phase generation process that maintains chemical validity at every step by operating on valid substructures","Demonstrates significant improvement in both validity and property optimization over state-of-the-art SMILES-based baselines"]}

methods: Junction Tree Variational Autoencoder (JT-VAE) for molecular graph generation
Two-phase generation: (1) generate a tree-structured scaffold (junction tree) of chemical substructures, (2) assemble subgraphs into a molecular graph using a graph message passing network
problems: Automated design of molecules with specific chemical properties
Continuous embedding and generation of molecular graphs
Direct generation of molecular graphs (as opposed to SMILES string generation)
Ensuring chemical validity during molecular graph generation
Efficient optimization of molecular properties
datasets: Not explicitly named in the provided content, but mentions 'training set' of molecules and standard datasets used in prior work (e.g., those used by Kusner et al., 2017; Dai et al., 2018)
metrics: Molecule validity (percentage of valid molecules generated)
Property optimization success (relative gain in discovering molecules with desired properties)

results.value: 100% valid molecules when sampled from prior
30% relative gain over baselines

novelty_claims: First direct generative model for molecular graphs using a junction tree representation, rather than SMILES strings
Two-phase generation process that maintains chemical validity at every step by operating on valid substructures
Demonstrates significant improvement in both validity and property optimization over state-of-the-art SMILES-based baselines
